questionText: Consider the statements $P:\ x^2 - 4x + 3 = 0$, $\ Q:\ x = 1$ and $\ R:\ x = 3$, where $x \in \mathbb{R}$. Investigate all the logical implications among $P$, $Q$ and $R$: establish whether $Q \Rightarrow P$, whether $R \Rightarrow P$, whether $P \Rightarrow (Q \vee R)$, and whether $P \Rightarrow Q$. Hence express the complete relationship between $P$ and the pair $Q,R$ using logical connectives, and comment on necessity and sufficiency.

steps.description: Lay out the three statements and the plan.
steps.workingLatex: P:\ x^2 - 4x + 3 = 0, \quad Q:\ x = 1, \quad R:\ x = 3
steps.explanation: Before deciding which arrows connect the statements, we record exactly what each one says. Because each symbol claims a direction, we will have to test both directions of every link separately.
Factorising exposes the roots directly, which is the fastest route to relating $P$ to the two candidate values $x=1$ and $x=3$.

steps.description: Rewrite P as a product equal to zero.
steps.explanation: Since the factorisation is an identity, the equation $P$ holds under exactly the same conditions as the product being zero. This is a genuine equivalence, not just an implication.
A product of real numbers is zero if and only if one of the factors is zero. This converts the algebra into a logical 'or' of the two value statements.

steps.description: Recognise the disjunction Q or R.
steps.explanation: The right-hand side is precisely the statement '$Q$ or $R$'. So already we can see the natural full relationship is a biconditional with the disjunction.

steps.description: Test Q implies P by substitution.
steps.explanation: Assuming $Q$ and putting $x=1$ into the quadratic gives zero, so $P$ holds. Hence $Q$ is enough on its own to guarantee $P$.

steps.description: Test R implies P by substitution.
steps.explanation: Assuming $R$ and putting $x=3$ into the quadratic also gives zero, so $P$ holds. Thus $R$ likewise guarantees $P$.

steps.description: Confirm P implies the disjunction Q or R.
steps.explanation: If $P$ holds, the factorised form forces one factor to vanish, giving $x=1$ or $x=3$. So $P$ cannot be true unless at least one of $Q$, $R$ is true.

steps.description: Test P implies Q with a counterexample.
steps.workingLatex: x = 3:\ P \text{ true}, \ Q \text{ false} \ \Rightarrow\ P \not\Rightarrow Q
steps.explanation: At $x=3$ the equation $P$ holds but $Q$ (which says $x=1$) is false. A single counterexample is enough to show $P$ does not force $Q$.

steps.description: Note the symmetric failure P implies R.
steps.workingLatex: x = 1:\ P \text{ true}, \ R \text{ false} \ \Rightarrow\ P \not\Rightarrow R
steps.explanation: By the mirror-image argument, $x=1$ satisfies $P$ but not $R$. So $P$ on its own pins the value down only to the pair, not to either one individually.

steps.description: Assemble both directions with the disjunction.
steps.explanation: Steps 6-7 give the disjunction implying $P$, and step 8 gives $P$ implying the disjunction. Both directions holding is exactly the condition for a biconditional.
Combining the two implications yields a clean equivalence between $P$ and 'either $Q$ or $R$'. This is the tightest true statement linking $P$ to the value statements.

steps.description: Translate into necessary and sufficient language.
steps.workingLatex: Q \text{ sufficient, not necessary, for } P; \quad (Q \vee R) \text{ necessary and sufficient for } P
steps.explanation: Each of $Q$ and $R$ guarantees $P$ so is sufficient, but neither is required (the other root works instead) so neither is necessary. The disjunction is both required and enough, so it is necessary and sufficient.

steps.workingLatex: Q \Rightarrow P, \ R \Rightarrow P, \ P \Rightarrow (Q \vee R), \ P \not\Rightarrow Q, \ P \iff (Q \vee R)
steps.explanation: Collecting the verified links gives a full picture: the individual values are one-way sufficient conditions, while their disjunction is the exact equivalent of $P$.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: $(x-1)(x-3)=0$ gives $P \iff (Q \vee R)$. Both $Q \Rightarrow P$ and $R \Rightarrow P$ hold, and $P \Rightarrow (Q \vee R)$, but $P \not\Rightarrow Q$ (since $x=3$ satisfies $P$). Each of $Q, R$ is sufficient but not necessary for $P$; $Q \vee R$ is necessary and sufficient for $P$.
